Front: Data passes schema, row-count, and null checks but predictions go haywire. Which validation axis catches it and how?
Back: Feature distribution. It looks inside each column for (a) invalid values (age < 0, a stray 'male' vs 'Male' category) and (b) distribution shift / training-serving skew, values individually valid but the distribution moved from training (e.g., a feature that was 20-40 now arrives 60-80). Structural checks cannot see either.

Front: What does a shadow release catch that a holdout cannot, and what is its cost?
Back: A holdout is static historical data; a shadow release runs the new model on REAL live traffic and reveals production behavior: latency under load, throughput, error rates, system stability. Crucially its outputs are NOT served to users (that is why it is safe). Cost: roughly double inference compute, and no true outcome signal since no one acts on the shadow predictions.

Shadow: real traffic in, outputs discarded, zero user exposure, observe operational metrics safely. Canary: expose a small slice of real users to the new model and watch outcomes. Canary risks user-facing harm; shadow does not.

Front: In a Dockerfile, why copy requirements.txt and pip install BEFORE copying app code?
Back: Layer caching. Changing a layer invalidates every layer beneath it. requirements change rarely (top), code changes often (bottom). So editing app code reuses the cached pip-install layer; only the COPY code layer and below rerun. One COPY . . would bust the install layer on every code edit.

Front: What is Docker's RUN layer cache keyed on, and why does that make version pinning matter?
Back: On the prior layers + the command string + any copied input files (e.g., requirements.txt), NOT on external network state. So if requirements.txt is unchanged, Docker reuses the cached pip layer even if a package changed upstream on PyPI. Unpinned deps can silently go stale; pinning (and --no-cache when needed) keeps builds deterministic.

Front: Valid Palindrome: where does the re.sub approach spend O(n) extra space, and how does two-pointer avoid it?
Back: re.sub returns a NEW cleaned string of size up to n (and s[::-1] adds another copy). The two-pointer version walks the ORIGINAL string in place with two integer indices = O(1) extra space; isalnum() skips non-alphanumerics inline so no cleaned copy is ever built.

Front: Two Sum II (sorted): why is it safe to permanently discard the left element when sum < target?
Back: Because L is paired with R, the largest remaining element, so L+R is the biggest sum L can make with anything left. If even that is below target, L + (any smaller element) is also below target, so L can never reach target and is discarded. The sort guarantees the current partner is the most extreme value, which licenses the elimination. O(n)/O(1).

Front: Why do versioned, pushed Docker images make rollbacks easy?
Back: A tagged image is an immutable, self-contained snapshot (code + deps + OS userland) stored in the registry. Rolling back = redeploy the previous tag (e.g., v2 -> v1), pull and run, no rebuild and no dependency re-resolution. Use real version tags (v1, v2, git SHA), not the mutable :latest. Caveat: image rolls back, but external state/DB changes do not.
